title: Medovník
subtitle: Czech layered honey cake with soured cream filling
excerpt: Medovník is a Czech layer cake made from eight thin honey-spiced pastry sheets baked separately, then layered with a soured cream and icing sugar filling. The assembled cake rests for at least 24 hours before serving; during that time the filling softens the layers from brittle biscuit into something yielding and fudge-like. It is a patient cake. The honey flavour sharpens on day two.
sourceNotes: Medovník is a staple of Czech and Slovak home baking, closely related to Russian medovik and the wider Central European tradition of thin honey-layer cakes. Recipes appear in Czech baking guides from the nineteenth century onwards. The name comes from med, the Czech word for honey. The cake is associated with Christmas and special occasions but is eaten year-round.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake must rest for at least 24 hours after assembly and improves further on day two. Keeps refrigerated for 5 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving an assembled layer cake at room temperature or in the fridge for 12 to 48 hours before slicing. The filling migrates into the layers, softening them and binding the whole cake together. Skipping this rest produces a cake that crumbles at the cut and has an uneven texture. The longer rest is particularly important for cakes with thin, crisp pastry layers such as medovník, Napoleon mille-feuille, and Hungarian kürtőskalács.

content.text: The dough is soft and warm when it comes off the heat; the honey, butter, and sugar are melted together before the flour and eggs go in. Work quickly while it is pliable. Chilled dough becomes brittle and cracks when rolled. Each layer is rolled on parchment, cut to size, then baked on the paper. They come out rigid and crisp; the

Put 150 g honey, 100 g butter, and 100 g sugar in a small saucepan over medium heat. Stir until melted and combined. Remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes. Beat in 2 large eggs, then add 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda and 1 tsp cinnamon. Stir in 400 g flour until a soft dough forms. Divide into 8 equal portions, each about 100 g. Roll each portion on a sheet of baking parchment to a circle about 22 cm across and 2 mm thick. Trim to a neat 20 cm circle using a plate as a guide. Keep the scraps.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Bake the layers on their parchment, one or two at a time, for 6 to 7 minutes each until golden and set. They will be rigid when cool. Gather all the scraps, bake them until golden, then crush finely for the crumb coating. Cool all layers completely on a rack.

Beat 600 g soured cream with 100 g sifted icing sugar and 1 tsp vanilla extract until smooth and thickened. Place the first layer on a serving plate. Spread a generous layer of filling (about 3 to 4 tablespoons) over it. Repeat with all eight layers, ending with a layer. Spread the remaining filling over the top and sides. Press the crushed biscuit crumbs onto the top and sides to coat. Refrigerate for at least 24 hours before slicing.

items.problem: Dough tears and cracks when rolled
items.solution: The dough cooled too much before rolling. It must be rolled while still warm and pliable. If it has firmed up, briefly warm the portion between your hands before rolling.

items.problem: Layers are still crisp and dry after assembly
items.solution: The cake has not rested for long enough. A minimum of 24 hours is required. At 48 hours the layers are fully softened. The filling needs time to penetrate the pastry.

items.problem: Cake collapses or slides when sliced
items.solution: The filling was too thin or the cake was not chilled before slicing. The filling should be thick enough to hold a trail when you draw a spoon through it. Chill the assembled cake for at least 1 hour before cutting.
